preamble: Velar palatalization of [k] to the postalveolar affricate [tʃ] is a widespread historical phonetic shift, typically triggered by following front vowels or the palatal glide [j]. This process, often involving an intermediate palatal stop stage [c], is a defining feature of the Slavic First Palatalization and the development of several Romance languages from Latin. In English, this is seen in the 'ch' of words like 'chin' (from Proto-Germanic *kinnus).

examples.note: Georgian undergoes velar palatalization before high vowels, historically and phonologically: /k/ > [tʃ] before [i], as seen in plural forms and diminutives.
Development before front vowels. E.g., Russian 'četyre' (four) from PIE *kwetwor-.
Old English palatalization before front vowels.

Turkish velar stops /k, ɡ/ are palatalized to [c, ɟ] when adjacent to front vowels /e, i/ in native words. E.g., kedi (cat) [ke.di], kız (girl) [kɯz] vs. gemi (ship) [ɡe.mi]. Also observable in diminutives and derivative affixes.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Schenker, A. M. (1995). The Dawn of Slavic.
Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Philology.
